one_line_takeaway: VALD cascades a near-free embedding-consistency check, a lightweight text-consistency check, and only-when-needed LLM-based response consolidation to defend LVLMs against misleading visual adversarial attacks, cutting worst-case runtime to under half of SmoothVLM's while beating it and the adversarially-trained FARE baseline on SentenceBERT captioning scores across MF-ii, MixAttack, and M-Attack.
key_finding: On MS-COCO captioning (Table 1), VALD outperforms both SmoothVLM variants across all three attacks and all three models, e.g. on LLaVA under MF-ii: VALD 0.716 SBERT vs. SmoothVLM-original 0.679 vs. SmoothVLM-Ours (same transforms as VALD) 0.703 vs. defense-off 0.268; clean-image performance is preserved (VALD 0.723 vs. defense-off 0.722 on LLaVA). Detection (Table 2): 93-96% of clean images correctly identified as clean (LLaVA 0.960, MiniGPT 0.954, Qwen3 0.930 accuracy on clean inputs), with recall on attacked inputs above 91% in every setting (e.g., Qwen3/MixAttack recall 0.912, the lowest reported). Against the training-based FARE baseline (Table 3, LLaVA, epsilon=16): VALD 0.716/0.701/0.673 vs. FARE 0.570/0.586/0.517 on MF-ii/MixAttack/M-Attack respectively, while FARE trails even the defense-off clean score (FARE clean 0.672 vs. off 0.722 vs. VALD 0.723). Runtime (Table 4/8): undefended captioning takes 1.8s/image; VALD best case (image filtered by early detector) is 1.9s/image; VALD worst case (full pipeline, LVLM querying 18.0s + late detector 0.2s + response generation 2.8s) is 22.9s/image; SmoothVLM (which always processes every image) takes 52.7s/image -- VALD is reported as 27x faster in the best case and 2x faster in the worst case. Ablations show the K=10 transformation mix is needed for robustness to adaptive/transformation-specific attacks (e.g., cropping alone collapses on M-Attack, SBERT 0.359, since M-Attack specifically targets crop-based defenses), and consolidation quality for M-Attack keeps improving with K up to about 10 (0.532 at K=4 to 0.673 at K=10) before diminishing.
core_contribution: VALD is a training-free, inference-time defense for Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) against visual 'misleading' adversarial attacks (perturbations that induce plausible-but-incorrect, non-jailbreak outputs). The paper's own contribution is a four-stage pipeline: (1) an early attack detector that compares the input image's vision-encoder embedding to embeddings of K=10 content-preserving transformations (large crop, pixel masking, JPEG compression, Gaussian blur) via a similarity threshold tau calibrated at the 95th percentile of clean-data distances, bypassing further processing for images deemed clean (paper reports ~95% of clean images are filtered here); (2) image-based response generation, querying the target LVLM K+1 times (original + K transforms); (3) a late-stage detector applying JailGuard's (Zhang et al. 2023) KL-divergence-over-similarity-distribution consistency check on the generated text responses, catching an additional ~1% of clean images; (4) an external LLM (Gemma-3-27B) that consolidates the K+1 textual responses into one final answer by keeping content corroborated across multiple responses and discarding singleton/inconsistent details -- notably this consolidation LLM never sees the image, only text. The authors frame their contribution against two prior training-free defenses: SmoothVLM (Sun et al. 2024), which randomly samples the final response from a majority-agreement group, and DPS/Zhou et al. 2024, which consolidates responses but requires access to the vision component during consolidation and depends on stronger LVLMs (e.g., GPT-4o-mini, Gemini). VALD's stated novelty is decoupling cheap visual querying (lightweight LVLM) from expensive text-only consolidation (invoked only for the minority of inputs flagged as suspicious), plus the two-stage cascade detector that lets most clean images skip expensive processing entirely.
threat_model: Attacker perturbs an input image (white-box vision-encoder access as in MF-ii and MixAttack, or black-box as in M-Attack) to bias an LVLM's caption/response toward a plausible but factually incorrect answer, without necessarily producing overtly harmful/toxic content (distinguished by the authors from jailbreaking attacks that target safety alignment). Adversary has gray-box access enabled by widespread reuse of public pretrained vision encoders like CLIP. Defender has white-box access to the target LVLM's vision encoder for the embedding-consistency check and can query an external LLM (Gemma-3-27B) for consolidation, but does not retrain or fine-tune the target model (training-free).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitation: the method assumes genuine semantic content is invariant under content-preserving transformations; when an error (e.g., hallucinated object) appears consistently across multiple transformed views, the consolidation LLM can converge on an incorrect but mutually-corroborated interpretation -- demonstrated with a real failure case where two motorcycles were hallucinated into three across most transformed views, producing an incorrect final consolidated caption. They also note an inherent efficiency/robustness trade-off: the more images the detector classifies as clean (to save compute), the higher the risk of missing an actual attack. Threshold calibration is dataset-distribution-dependent: thresholds tuned on COCO increase false-positive flagging (more clean images sent to the expensive pipeline) when applied to Flickr8k Expert, though the authors argue this only costs compute, not captioning accuracy, since misclassified-clean images still get correctly-defended captions. Detection precision degrades sharply as the proportion of truly clean images grows (Table 9: precision drops from ~0.96 at 50% clean to ~0.56-0.57 at 95% clean), a base-rate effect the paper acknowledges. Evaluation is restricted to compact/mid-size open LVLMs (7B/4B and MiniGPT-v2) and to captioning as the task; not evaluated against jailbreaking/safety-alignment attacks (explicitly out of scope) or against attacks adaptive to VALD's own detection/consolidation mechanism itself.

datasets_used: MS-COCO (1,000 images sampled from validation split, target attack texts from training split)
Flickr8k Expert (100 images from training split, secondary evaluation in Appendix H)
quantitative_results: SBERT captioning, LLaVA/MF-ii: VALD 0.716 vs SmoothVLM-original 0.679 vs SmoothVLM-Ours 0.703 vs defense-off 0.268 (Table 1)
SBERT captioning, LLaVA/clean: VALD 0.723 vs defense-off 0.722 (Table 1)
Detection accuracy on clean images: LLaVA 0.960, MiniGPT 0.954, Qwen3 0.930 (Table 2)
Detection recall on attacks: minimum 0.912 (Qwen3/MixAttack), maximum 0.999 (MiniGPT/MF-ii) (Table 2)
VALD vs FARE (LLaVA, eps=16): MF-ii 0.716 vs 0.570; MixAttack 0.701 vs 0.586; M-Attack 0.673 vs 0.517; Clean 0.723 vs 0.672 (Table 3)
Runtime: defense-off 1.8s/image; VALD best case 1.9s/image; VALD worst case 22.9s/image; SmoothVLM (always full) 52.7s/image -> 27x faster best case, 2x faster worst case (Table 4)
Early detector alone filters ~95% of clean images; late detector adds ~0.5-1% more (Section 4.1/4.2)
Detection precision vs. clean-image proportion (MF-ii): 0.962 precision at 50% clean vs 0.568 precision at 95% clean, with recall >=0.998 in both cases (Table 9)
baselines_compared: SmoothVLM original (Sun et al. 2024) - random pixel masking + majority-group random sampling
SmoothVLM-Ours variant - same transformation set as VALD, otherwise SmoothVLM's random-sample consolidation
FARE (Schlarmann et al. 2024) - adversarially fine-tuned CLIP vision backbone, training-based defense
DPS/Zhou et al. 2024 discussed but excluded from head-to-head captioning comparison because it requires stronger LVLMs (GPT-4o-mini, Gemini) not applicable to the compact models tested
